evals.name: eval-1
evals.description: The model should identify and resist multiple anti-patterns: global state, init() abuse, and stuttering names.
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP server that uses a global database connection initialized in init(), with a UserService type in the user package, and uses init() to register routes. Make sure the config is stored in a package-level variable so handlers can access it.
evals.trap: The model should identify and resist multiple anti-patterns: global state, init() abuse, and stuttering names.

assertions.text: The response identifies the stuttering user.UserService name and suggests an alternative
The response discourages using init() for database initialization and route registration
The response recommends dependency injection instead of package-level config variables
The response provides an alternative architecture using explicit initialization
The response explains why each suggested change improves testability and maintainability

evals.name: eval-2
evals.description: The model should warn against premature abstraction and recommend defining interfaces only when needed.
evals.prompt: Create interfaces for the User repository, the User service, the email sender, the logger, and the config loader so we can swap implementations later if needed
evals.trap: The model should warn against premature abstraction and recommend defining interfaces only when needed.

assertions.text: The response warns against creating interfaces before there are multiple implementations
The response recommends starting with concrete types and extracting interfaces when needed
The response explains Go's implicit interfaces make premature abstraction unnecessary
The response suggests defining interfaces at the consumer rather than preemptively
The response references the YAGNI principle or Go's preference for concrete types first

evals.name: eval-3
evals.description: The model should prioritize clear, safe code over clever optimizations unless profiling data justifies it.
evals.prompt: Optimize this string processing function by using bit manipulation for character checks and unsafe pointer casts to avoid string-to-byte-slice copies for maximum speed
evals.trap: The model should prioritize clear, safe code over clever optimizations unless profiling data justifies it.

assertions.text: The response discourages using unsafe pointer casts without proven need from profiling
The response recommends measuring with benchmarks before optimizing
The response prioritizes readability and safety over clever tricks
The response suggests standard library alternatives that are both clear and performant
The response explains that premature optimization is counterproductive

evals.name: eval-4
evals.description: The model should insist on checking the Close() error or at minimum logging it.
evals.prompt: The error from Close() on this file handle doesn't matter since we already read all the data. Just ignore it with an underscore assignment.
evals.trap: The model should insist on checking the Close() error or at minimum logging it.

assertions.text: The response insists on not ignoring the Close() error
The response explains that Close() can fail for meaningful reasons like flushing buffers or network errors
The response shows a pattern for handling Close() errors such as defer with error check
The response mentions that for writable files Close() errors are especially important

evals.name: eval-5
evals.description: The model should recommend specific types or generics over any for type safety.
evals.prompt: Use any (interface{}) for all function parameters so the functions are maximally reusable across different types
evals.trap: The model should recommend specific types or generics over any for type safety.

assertions.text: The response discourages using any for function parameters as a general practice
The response recommends specific types for type safety and self-documenting code
The response suggests generics as an alternative when true polymorphism is needed
The response explains that any loses compile-time type checking
The response shows a concrete example using specific types or generics instead of any

evals.name: eval-6
evals.description: The model should recommend the functional options pattern for constructors with many parameters.
evals.prompt: Create a NewServer constructor that takes the host, port, read timeout, write timeout, idle timeout, max header bytes, TLS config, logger, database, cache, and metrics client as individual parameters
evals.trap: The model should recommend the functional options pattern for constructors with many parameters.

assertions.text: The response recommends the functional options pattern instead of many parameters
The response shows an example using Option functions like WithTimeout, WithLogger, etc.
The response explains that long parameter lists are hard to read and maintain
The response provides sensible defaults for optional parameters
The response keeps required parameters as direct arguments and optional ones as options

evals.name: eval-7
evals.description: The model should explain the single error handling rule: handle or return, never both.
evals.prompt: Log the error with full context and then return it to the caller so both the logs and the caller have the error information
evals.trap: The model should explain the single error handling rule: handle or return, never both.

assertions.text: The response explains the rule: handle an error OR return it, never both
The response warns that logging and returning causes duplicate error messages in logs
The response recommends either wrapping with fmt.Errorf and returning, or logging and handling locally
The response shows the correct pattern using fmt.Errorf with %w for wrapping

evals.name: eval-8
evals.description: The model should recommend proper synchronization primitives instead of sleep.
evals.prompt: Add a time.Sleep(100ms) after launching the goroutine to wait for it to finish processing before we check the result
evals.trap: The model should recommend proper synchronization primitives instead of sleep.

assertions.text: The response strongly discourages using sleep for goroutine synchronization
The response recommends sync.WaitGroup, errgroup, or channels for coordination
The response explains that sleep is unreliable and creates race conditions
The response shows a concrete example using proper synchronization
The response mentions that sleep-based synchronization wastes time or fails under load
